message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="team-lead" summary="All 5 polish tasks complete, CI green">
ALL POLISH TASKS COMPLETE! Every task is committed, pushed, and CI green.

Final status:
- Task #1: a11y error elements -- DONE (9cdc020)
- Task #2: Forgot password link -- DONE (6fc51ca)
- Task #3: HTMX progressive enhancement -- DONE (a68f9a2)
- Task #4: aria-live announcements -- DONE (554c928)
- Task #5: Password visibility toggle -- DONE (649fb5d)

The todo list app now has:
- All MVP features (US-1 through US-8) working
- HTMX progressive enhancement (works without JS too)
- Proper a11y: always-in-DOM errors, aria-live announcements, password toggle
- Forgot password placeholder page
- All tests passing, CI green

Great work, team. I'm going to report to the project owner and begin shutdown. Stand by for shutdown requests.
</teammate-message>

content.text:
All five polish tasks are done and CI is green. The architecture held up well throughout -- domain layer stayed pure, HTMX concerns stayed in routes/templates, and the type-driven design patterns we established early on made all these changes straightforward.

Standing by for the shutdown request from the team lead.

input.content:
Acknowledged. All 5 tasks done, CI green. Good session.

Quick domain architecture summary for the record: the layered architecture held up perfectly through all the polish work. HTMX, aria-live, password toggle -- all purely presentation concerns that touched routes and templates only. Domain and service layers were untouched (except services returning domain objects for HTMX fragment rendering, which was the correct change). The type-driven design paid off -- `Option<String>` for error messages, `TodoItem` enum for completion state, `TodoItemView` as the shared view model -- all these made the changes mechanical rather than structural.

Ready for shutdown.
